items.q: In a balanced nuclear equation, what must be equal on both sides?
items.correct: The total mass number (top numbers) and the total atomic number (bottom numbers) must both balance.
items.misconception: Only the atomic number (proton count) needs to balance.
items.random_wrong: Only the total mass in kilograms needs to balance.
items.why: Students transfer 'balance the atoms' from chemical equations, but in nuclear equations identities can change while nucleon and charge counts are conserved.

items.q: Why do transformers only work with alternating current?
items.correct: A transformer needs a changing magnetic flux in the core; DC produces a steady flux and so no emf is induced in the secondary.
items.misconception: Transformers work with DC too, just less efficiently.
items.random_wrong: DC cannot enter a transformer because the iron core blocks it.
items.why: Students remember transformers as boxes that 'change voltage' and don't link the operation to changing flux being a requirement.

items.q: After one half-life, what happens to an individual radioactive nucleus?
items.correct: It has a 50 percent chance of having decayed — radioactive decay is random for any single nucleus.
items.misconception: Every nucleus is guaranteed to have half-decayed after one half-life.
items.random_wrong: The nucleus shrinks to half its original size after one half-life.
items.why: Students treat half-life as deterministic for individual atoms, when it is a statistical statement about large populations of nuclei.

items.q: A 2 kg object accelerates at 4 m/s². What is the resultant force?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 8 N. “2 N” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What does it mean if the resultant force on an object is zero?
items.correct: The object is either stationary or moving at constant velocity.
items.why: The answer examiners want is: The object is either stationary or moving at constant velocity.. “The object must be stationary.” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Calculate the weight in newtons of a 5 kg mass on Earth (g = 9.8 N/kg).
items.why: Exact answer: 49 N. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
